ete-q1: GPUs use thousands of small cores for parallel visual computations, not general data storage.
ete-q2: pinMode() is the correct ESP32 function to set GPIO pin mode; digitalMode() doesn't exist.
ete-q3: WiFi.begin() is the correct ESP32 function to initiate Wi-Fi connection; WiFi.connect() is not an ESP32 API.
ete-q4: The Control Unit manages instruction sequencing; the ALU performs arithmetic/logic operations.
ete-q5: setup() runs once at power-up; loop() executes repeatedly after setup completes.
ete-q6: Raspberry Pi OS is a full desktop OS that doesn't support ESP32 development like Arduino IDE does.
ete-q7: digitalWrite(4, HIGH) turns the LED on and it stays on because loop() is empty—no toggling logic.
ete-q8: WiFi.h must be included for WiFi functions; ESP32.h is for generic ESP32-specific features.
ete-q9: Supervised learning requires labeled input-output pairs; unsupervised finds patterns without labels.
ete-q10: Unsupervised learning (like clustering) finds hidden patterns without labeled data.
ete-q11: Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier, not typically used in reinforcement learning's trial-and-error framework.
ete-q12: The fundamental unit in quantum computing is a qubit (quantum bit), not a classical bit.
ete-q13: Superposition allows a qubit to exist in both 0 and 1 states simultaneously.
ete-q14: Entanglement is a quantum phenomenon where qubits become correlated; superposition is different.
ete-q15: The qubit (quantum bit) is the foundation of quantum computing's exponential advantage.
ete-q16: Quantum cryptography is a direct application of quantum computing principles.
ete-q17: The bit (binary digit) is the fundamental unit of classical information.
ete-q18: When WiFi.begin() fails, it returns WL_CONNECTED (which is not 1) — error codes are used.
ete-q19: Basic calculations (calculator) don't require AI; AI involves learning and decision-making.
ete-q20: GPUs excel at parallel processing tasks like image rendering, not general-purpose sequential tasks.
ete-q21: SMT (Surface Mount Technology) largely replaced THT (Through-Hole Technology) for compact devices.
ete-q22: SMT (Surface Mount Technology) places components directly on the PCB surface.
ete-q23: SMT allows higher component density and placement on both PCB sides compared to through-hole.
ete-q24: In SMD sizing, 0603 means 0.06in x 0.03in (1.6mm x 0.8mm metric) — dimensions are in hundredths of an inch.
ete-q25: A DFM (Design for Manufacturability) check evaluates design specifications for production readiness.
ete-q26: A mechanical fixture called a guide plate holds the PCB during solder paste printing.
ete-q27: AOI (Automated Optical Inspection) efficiently inspects larger PCB batches using machine vision.
ete-q28: Cobots (collaborative robots) enable safe human-robot collaboration in smart manufacturing.
ete-q29: Robots in smart manufacturing perform pick-and-place, assembly, welding, painting, and machining.
ete-q30: Green manufacturing addresses growing e-waste by using sustainable, eco-friendly processes.
ete-q31: WEEE (Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment) is the EU directive for electronic waste management.
ete-q32: RoHS and similar standards are used for certifying manufacturing material safety and restriction of hazardous substances.
ete-q33: EPEAT evaluates products into Bronze, Silver, and Gold tiers of environmental performance.
ete-q34: EPEAT does not audit medical equipment categories under its certification framework.
ete-q35: KiCad is a popular open-source PCB design tool used in open-source hardware development.
ete-q36: SkyWater 130nm is an example of an open-source PDK (Process Design Kit) for chip fabrication.
ete-q37: KiCad is used for open-source circuit simulation and PCB design.
ete-q38: Articulated robots with multiple joints perform complex 3D movements like welding and painting.
ete-q39: SCARA robots are mainly used for pick-and-place, assembly, and precision operations.
ete-q40: AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robots) use sensors to navigate factory floors independently.
ete-q41: NGN architecture has 4 layers: transport, control, service/control, and application.
ete-q42: The management interface in NGN does not support media interaction — it's for administration only.
ete-q43: OTN (Optical Transport Network) supports bitrates up to 100 Gbps and beyond per wavelength.
ete-q44: 5.5G (5G-Advanced) builds upon 5G standards as defined by 3GPP Release 18.
ete-q45: NGN layers include the transport layer, service/control layer, and application layer.
ete-q46: CDF (Content Delivery Function) in NGN operates within the service/control layer.
ete-q47: Media Gateways are located in the transport layer of NGN architecture.
ete-q48: TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access) is the multiplexing scheme used in 3G mobile networks.
ete-q49: FM Broadcast operates in the 88–108 MHz frequency range worldwide.
ete-q50: MPLS label header is 32 bits: 20-bit label, 8-bit TTL, 3-bit TC, 1-bit bottom-of-stack.
ete-q51: STM-1 carries 8000 frames per second at 155.52 Mbps; each frame takes 125 μs.
ete-q52: EXP (Experimental) bits in MPLS are reserved for experimental or quality-of-service marking.
ete-q53: 5G theoretical peak data speeds reach up to 10 Gbps under ideal conditions.
ete-q54: SDH (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy) is the international standard for fiber-optic transmission.
ete-q55: TTL (Time To Live) in an MPLS label is an 8-bit field that prevents routing loops.
ete-q56: In FTTH architecture, telemedicine is classified as an enhanced Tier-3 service.
ete-q57: OLT (Optical Line Terminal) is critical in PON (Passive Optical Network) fiber-to-the-home architecture.
ete-q58: OLT's primary function is to manage and coordinate multiple ONUs in the PON network.
ete-q59: The protection scheme in OTN is defined by network management system (NMS) provisioning.
ete-q60: SDH provides features like synchronous multiplexing, clock synchronization, and network management.
ete-q61: The network layer (perception layer) in IoT handles data transmission between devices and the internet.
ete-q62: Physical system monitoring is a valid CPS function; the incorrect option claims it isn't.
ete-q63: Cloud services like AWS IoT Core provide specialized platforms for IoT data visualization.
ete-q64: Industry 5.0 focuses on human-machine collaboration and personalization over full automation.
ete-q65: MQTT is a lightweight publish-subscribe IoT protocol; HTTP is too heavyweight for many IoT devices.
ete-q66: AR (Augmented Reality) overlays digital information onto the real world in real-time.
ete-q67: Precision farming and smart agriculture ARE IoT applications; desktop publishing is not.
ete-q68: Industry 4.0 introduced automation and computers; Industry 3.0 introduced electronics and IT.
ete-q69: AWS (Amazon Web Services) leads in AI/ML and data analytics among major cloud providers.
ete-q70: Extended Reality (XR) encompasses VR (Virtual Reality), AR (Augmented Reality), and MR (Mixed Reality).
ete-q71: IIoT applies IoT technologies specifically to industrial environments for monitoring, control, and optimization.
ete-q72: Human-centric design and sustainability distinguish Industry 5.0 from Industry 4.0's focus on full automation.
ete-q73: Spatial Mapping creates 3D digital representations of physical environments for AR/VR applications.
ete-q74: IoT improves manufacturing efficiency through real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and process automation.
ete-q75: Desktop publishing is unrelated to Industry 4.0's smart manufacturing and cyber-physical systems focus.
ete-q76: The middleware layer in a 5-layer IoT architecture provides communication and integration between layers.
ete-q77: Industry 5.0 aims to augment human workers with cobots, unlike Industry 4.0's push toward full automation.
ete-q78: In Industry 5.0, the collaboration layer is at the core where humans and cobots work together.
ete-q79: Mass production focus is excluded from Industry 5.0 benefits, which prioritize customization and sustainability.
ete-q80: Google Cloud IoT is chosen for predictive maintenance due to its strong ML and data analytics capabilities.
ete-q81: Multi-rotor drones (quadcopters) can hover precisely over small areas for aerial photography.
ete-q82: ESC (Electronic Speed Controller) precisely regulates motor speed for smooth drone flight control.
ete-q83: IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) with accelerometer and gyroscope provides altitude and orientation data.
ete-q84: The gimbal stabilization system uses motors to maintain stable camera orientation during drone flight.
ete-q85: DGCA categorizes Indian airspace into Red (no-drone), Yellow (controlled), and Green (free) zones.
ete-q86: DGCA (Directorate General of Civil Aviation) is India's regulatory authority for drone licensing and safety.
ete-q87: All drones except Nano category (<250g) must be registered on India's Digital Sky platform.
ete-q88: The Power Distribution Board (PDB) routes electrical power from battery to ESCs and other components.

ete-q90: Heavy-lift octocopters with multiple motors can deliver substantial payloads to remote areas.
ete-q91: VTOL (Vertical Take-Off and Landing) drones suit vaccine delivery to villages with limited landing infrastructure.

ete-q95: Maximum current = 2.2Ah × 25C = 55A; with a 3S (11.1V) battery, max power ≈ 610W.
ete-q96: Fixed-wing drones with thermal cameras provide long endurance for dense forest wildlife tracking.
ete-q97: VTOL drones combine multi-rotor precision with fixed-wing efficiency for medical supply delivery.
ete-q98: Surveillance drones require long endurance, thermal imaging, and stable flight for critical infrastructure monitoring.
ete-q99: ESC (Electronic Speed Controller) precisely regulates motor speed for stable payload transport.
ete-q100: Rural open areas up to 120m AGL without prior approval fall under India's Green Zone category.
